Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

4 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path AI/ML
End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → LLD foundations and prompt framing

LLD learning path

LLD foundations and prompt framing

Learn how to turn a machine-coding or object-design prompt into bounded requirements, clean responsibilities, and explicit object contracts.

Module progress 0%

0 of 3 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: LLD prompt framing and scope control

Start low-level design interviews by defining the first version, edge cases, and non-negotiable invariants before naming classes.

Why this module matters Design responsibilities cleanly before patterns start to stack up.

Move through the lessons in order so each object-model decision has context before follow-up requirements and machine-coding trade-offs appear.

Study target 3 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

Low-level design interview prep 3 lessons 0/3 complete Follow in order for best results Back to LLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the prompt shape, object boundaries, and extension seams before coding anything.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
LLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Clarify scope, actors, and invariants before sketching classes
  • Model responsibilities so each type owns one coherent reason to change
  • Choose interfaces and APIs that survive follow-up requirements

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core object or workflow decision in your own words.
  • Sketch the key classes, responsibilities, and extension seams before you code.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions, invariants, and object boundaries.
  • Justified abstraction choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of follow-ups such as testing, concurrency, and extensibility.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

Responsibilities, interfaces, and seams

Assign behavior to the right object, define collaboration boundaries, and keep business rules testable without framework noise.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Validation, errors, and state transitions

Design methods that enforce invariants, express failure paths cleanly, and keep state changes easy to reason about.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

LLD prompt framing and scope control

Open

Start low-level design interviews by defining the first version, edge cases, and non-negotiable invariants before naming classes.

Why it matters

Weak LLD answers jump into class diagrams too early. Strong ones narrow the problem with actors, responsibilities, constraints, and failure rules first.

What you will cover

Clarify the product sliceTranslate rules into invariantsChoose the first design boundary

Good interview signal

  • State the scope of version one before drawing types.
  • List the invariants the design must preserve.
20-30 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Responsibilities, interfaces, and seams

Open

Assign behavior to the right object, define collaboration boundaries, and keep business rules testable without framework noise.

Why it matters

Interviewers look for designs where responsibilities are obvious, dependencies are controlled, and logic remains testable as the prompt evolves.

What you will cover

Assign ownership deliberatelyDesign collaboration seamsKeep the API readable

Good interview signal

  • Explain why each core class exists.
  • Name the interface boundaries that isolate external dependencies.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Validation, errors, and state transitions

Open

Design methods that enforce invariants, express failure paths cleanly, and keep state changes easy to reason about.

Why it matters

Low-level design quality shows up in how safely objects move between states, reject invalid commands, and explain failure without leaking inconsistent state.

What you will cover

Model valid transitionsChoose an error strategyProtect invariants under change

Good interview signal

  • Describe the legal state machine for the core object.
  • Show how invalid commands are rejected.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
